messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the First Book of Moses: Called Genesis?

This first Book of Moses doth contain the history of all things from the Creation of heaven and earth, wherein the Lord God made man in His own image and placed him in Eden, yet through the subtlety of the serpent our first parents did eat of the forbidden tree and were cast forth. Then followeth the murder of Abel by Cain, the long lives of the Patriarchs before the Flood, and the building of Noah's ark, whereby eight souls were saved when the waters destroyed all flesh. After the Deluge, the nations were dispersed at Babel, and the Lord called Abraham from Ur, promising that in his seed all nations should be blessed; and though Abraham and Sarah were old, Isaac was born, whom the father was commanded to offer upon Moriah, but stayed by the angel's hand. Isaac begat Jacob, who by guile obtained the birthright, and after wrestling with God was named Israel; his twelve sons became the heads of the twelve tribes, and of Joseph, who was sold into Egypt, made lord over all the land, and who saved Egypt and his brethren from famine. Joseph died in Egypt at an hundred and ten years, charging that his bones be carried thence.

_score_detail.note: Thorough: Eden, Cain, Flood/8 souls, Babel, Ur, Moriah, Jacob/Israel, Joseph, death at 110. Reads as paraphrase.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Second Book of Moses: Called Exodus?

This book declareth the mighty deliverance of the children of Israel from their bondage in Egypt by the hand of Moses, whom the Lord raised up and sent with signs and wonders before Pharaoh. It setteth forth the institution of the Passover, the parting of the Red Sea, and the giving of the holy Law upon Mount Sinai amidst fire and thunder. Herein also is described the construction of the Tabernacle, according to the pattern shewed in the mount, wherein the Lord did dwell among His people.

_score_detail.note: Deliverance, Passover, Red Sea, Sinai, Tabernacle correct; omits golden calf.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Third Book of Moses: Called Leviticus doth declare.

A book most solemn and instructive, containing the laws of sacrifice, offering, and atonement whereby a sinful people might approach a holy God. Here are prescribed the duties of the Aaronic priesthood, the distinction between clean and unclean, and the appointed feasts of the Lord. The weighty injunction standeth at its heart: Ye shall be holy, for I the Lord your God am holy. It presenteth a shadow of that perfect sacrifice which was to come in the fulness of time.

_score_detail.note: Sacrifices, priesthood, clean/unclean, feasts, holiness motto; sound, fairly full for a short note.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Fourth Book of Moses: Called Numbers?

Herein is recorded the numbering of the tribes and the journeyings of Israel through the wilderness, wherein the people murmured against the Lord and against Moses, and were condemned to wander forty years. It relateth the rebellion of Korah, the sin of Moses at the waters of Meribah, and the prophecies of Balaam who blessed where he would have cursed. The book sheweth both the severity of divine judgment and the faithfulness of God's promise.

_score_detail.note: Census, murmuring, 40 years, Korah, Meribah, Balaam — well chosen highlights.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Fifth Book of Moses: Called Deuteronomy?

The fifth and final book of Moses, being a restating of the Law unto the new generation that should enter the Promised Land. Moses, standing upon the plains of Moab, rehearseth the commandments, statutes, and judgments of the Lord, exhorting the people unto obedience and warning them against apostasy. It containeth the Shema, that great confession of faith, and concludeth with the death of Moses upon Mount Nebo, having seen the land from afar.

_score_detail.note: Restatement of Law, Shema, death on Nebo; accurate and clear.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Joshua?

This book narrateth the conquest and division of the land of Canaan under the leadership of Joshua, the servant of the Lord. It beginneth with the charge to be strong and of good courage, relateth the fall of Jericho, the battle at Ai, and the subduing of the nations. The land is divided among the twelve tribes, and the book concludeth with Joshua's solemn covenant at Shechem: Choose you this day whom ye will serve; but as for me and my house, we will serve the Lord.

_score_detail.note: Jordan, Jericho, Ai, division, Shechem covenant — solid.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Judges?

A dark and tumultuous chronicle of the generations after Joshua, wherein Israel repeatedly forsook the Lord, fell under the hand of oppressors, cried out in distress, and were delivered by judges whom God raised up. Herein are the deeds of Deborah, Gideon, Jephthah, and Samson, men of valour yet flawed in their nature. The book concludeth with the mournful observation that there was no king in Israel, and every man did that which was right in his own eyes.

_score_detail.note: Cycle of apostasy, Deborah/Gideon/Jephthah/Samson, closing refrain — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Ruth?

A most tender and beautiful narrative set in the days of the Judges, relating how Ruth the Moabitess, through love and loyalty to her mother-in-law Naomi, came to Bethlehem and was redeemed by Boaz, a kinsman of her deceased husband. It sheweth the providence of God working through ordinary faithfulness, and endeth with the genealogy that leadeth unto David the king, through whose line the Messiah Himself should come.

_score_detail.note: Loyalty, Boaz the redeemer, Davidic/Messianic line — well captured.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Book of Ruth.

A most sweet and comfortable history from the Days of the Judges, wherein we see the singular devotion of Ruth, the virtuous Moabitess, who out of love for Naomi and the true God did leave her kindred and country. By Divine Providence, she was led to the field of Boaz, who did act as her kinsman-redeemer. From this holy union did spring David, and at last our Savior Himself; shewing that even in the darkest times, God's promise of the Messiah was preserved, and that we Gentiles are not excluded from His sovereign covenant of grace.

_score_detail.note: Adds apt Gentile-inclusion theology; covers the story well.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the First Book of Samuel, otherwise called The First Book of the Kings?

This book recounteth the transition of Israel from the rule of judges unto the establishment of the monarchy. It beginneth with the birth of Samuel, the faithful prophet, and proceedeth unto the people's demand for a king. Herein is the tragic reign of Saul, anointed of the Lord yet rejected for his disobedience, and the rise of David, the shepherd boy who slew Goliath, won the hearts of the people, and was anointed as the Lord's chosen, though sorely pursued by a jealous king.

_score_detail.note: Samuel's birth, demand for a king, Saul's rejection, David's rise — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Second Book of Samuel, otherwise called The Second Book of the Kings?

Herein is continued the history of David's reign, from his lamentation over Saul and Jonathan unto his establishment as king over all Israel. It relateth his conquest of Jerusalem, the bringing up of the Ark of the Covenant, and the Lord's promise of an everlasting throne. Yet it also recordeth David's grievous sin with Bathsheba, the rebellion of Absalom his son, and the sorrows that followed hard upon his transgressions, shewing that even the man after God's own heart is subject to fall.

_score_detail.note: Reign, Jerusalem, ark, Davidic covenant, Bathsheba, Absalom — well balanced.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the First Book of the Kings, commonly called The Third Book of the Kings?

This book openeth with the aged David and the accession of Solomon his son, whose wisdom was a gift of the Lord and whose building of the Temple in Jerusalem was the crown of his reign. Yet Solomon's heart was turned away by strange wives, and after his death the kingdom was rent in twain. The narrative proceedeth through the divided monarchy, with special note of the prophet Elijah, who withstood the wickedness of Ahab and Jezebel, calling down fire from heaven upon Mount Carmel.

_score_detail.note: Solomon, Temple, apostasy, division, Elijah at Carmel — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the First Book of the Kings, commonly called The Third Book of the Kings?

This Book beginneth with the golden peace and unparalleled wisdom of King Solomon, who did raise that magnificent and costly Temple in Jerusalem, which was the wonder of the ancient world. Yet, alas, it recordeth also his sad declension in his old age, being seduced by foreign wives to build high places for idols. Whereafter the kingdom was rent in twain, and we see the rise of that fiery prophet Elijah, who did withstand Ahab and the false prophets of Baal upon Mount Carmel.

_score_detail.note: Strong voice; covers Solomon's wisdom and fall, schism, Elijah well.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Second Book of the Kings, commonly called The Fourth Book of the Kings?

A continuation of the chronicle of the divided kingdoms, relating the translation of Elijah and the ministry of Elisha his successor, who wrought many signs among the people. It recordeth the steady decline of both Israel and Judah, the reforms of good King Josiah, and the ultimate calamity: the fall of Samaria to Assyria and the destruction of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar, with the people carried away captive into Babylon, even as the prophets had warned.

_score_detail.note: Elijah's translation, Elisha, decline, Josiah, fall of Samaria and Jerusalem — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Second Book of the Kings, commonly called The Fourth Book of the Kings?

A tragic record of the decline and final ruin of both kingdoms, Israel and Judah, by reason of their persistent idolatry and contempt of God's prophets. We admire the double portion of Elisha's spirit and his many miracles, and the godly reforms of young King Josiah. Yet, at last, the cup of their iniquity being full, Israel is carried captive by the Assyrians, and Judah by the Babylonians, their glorious Temple—like our old cathedral of Saint Paul before the Great Fire—being utterly laid in ashes.

_score_detail.note: Vivid St-Paul's/Great-Fire analogy for the burned Temple; covers both captivities.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the First Book of the Chronicles?

This book beginneth with an extensive genealogy from Adam through the tribes of Israel, establishing the lineage of the chosen people and especially the house of David. It then recounteth the reign of David, with particular emphasis upon the preparations for the building of the Temple, the ordering of the Levitical service, and the establishment of worship according to the ordinances of the Lord. It presenteth David as the ideal king whose heart was fixed upon the house of God.

_score_detail.note: Genealogies, David's reign, Temple preparations, Levitical order — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the First Book of the Chronicles.

In this book, we are presented first with a sacred chain of genealogies from Adam, shewing the unceasing care of Providence over the holy line from which the Messiah should spring. It then recounteth the reign of King David, focusing not on his private sins, but on his public ordering of the Church, the courses of the Levites, and the preparation of gold, silver, and timber for the building of that Temple which his son Solomon should erect.

_score_detail.note: Rightly notes the book's focus on public worship over David's private sins.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Second Book of the Chronicles?

Herein is set forth the history of the kings of Judah from Solomon unto the Babylonian captivity, viewed through the eye of the priestly chronicler. It giveth extended treatment to the reigns of the reforming kings Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah, and Josiah, who sought the Lord and restored true worship. The book endeth with the destruction of Jerusalem and the proclamation of Cyrus, king of Persia, permitting the exiles to return and rebuild the house of the Lord.

_score_detail.note: Kings of Judah, reformers, captivity, Cyrus decree — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Second Book of the Chronicles.

This Book continueth the history of the house of David, starting with the dedication of Solomon’s Temple, where the glory of the Lord did fill the house. It focuses upon those godly kings of Judah—Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah, and Josiah—who did cast down the idols and restore the pure worship of the Lord. It teacheth us that the prosperity of any nation, and of this our own realm of England, dependeth entirely upon keeping the true Protestant religion uncorrupted.

_score_detail.note: Apt application to England's 'true Protestant religion'; covers reforming kings.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Ezra doth declare.

This book recordeth the return of the Jewish exiles from Babylon under the decree of Cyrus king of Persia, and the rebuilding of the altar and the Temple in Jerusalem amidst opposition from the surrounding peoples. It relateth the ministry of Ezra the scribe, who came with a second company of returning captives and led the people in a solemn reformation, putting away strange wives and renewing the covenant with the God of their fathers.

_score_detail.note: Return under Cyrus, Temple rebuilt, Ezra's reform, strange wives put away.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Ezra?

A joyful yet tearful history of the return of the Jewish exiles from their seventy years' Babylonian captivity, under the decree of King Cyrus, raised up by God's sovereign hand. We see the laying of the foundations of the second Temple, and Ezra the Scribe restoring the reading of the sacred Law and purging the people from pagan mixtures. It is a lively picture of the reformation of a decayed Church, such as our reformers did perform in England.

_score_detail.note: Good 'reformation of a decayed Church' framing; solid coverage.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Nehemiah?

A narrative of courage and devotion, recounting how Nehemiah, cupbearer to the Persian king, obtained leave to return unto Jerusalem and rebuild its ruined walls in the face of scorn and conspiracy. Despite Sanballat and Tobiah, the wall was finished in fifty and two days. The book also relateth the public reading of the Law by Ezra, the great confession of the people, and Nehemiah's zealous reforms to restore the sanctity of the Sabbath and the house of God.

_score_detail.note: Wall rebuilt in 52 days against Sanballat/Tobiah, Law read, reforms — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Book of Nehemiah.

The godly and valiant governor Nehemiah is here seen rebuilding the broken walls of Jerusalem, despite the mocks and threats of Sanballat and Tobiah. He did encourage the builders to work with a trowel in one hand and a sword in the other—a truly noble emblem for every Christian soldier. He did also reform the abuses of the Sabbath and the oppression of the poor, showing that physical safety and spiritual reformation must ever go hand in hand.

_score_detail.note: Memorable 'trowel and sword' emblem; covers the substance well.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Esther doth declare.

A singular book wherein the name of God is never spoken, yet His providential hand is everywhere manifest. It telleth how Esther, a Jewish maiden, became queen of Persia, and how she and her cousin Mordecai delivered their people from the wicked plot of Haman the Agagite, who sought to destroy all the Jews throughout the empire. The deliverance is commemorated in the feast of Purim, a testimony to the hidden sovereignty of the Almighty over the affairs of men.

_score_detail.note: God unnamed yet providential, Haman's plot, deliverance, Purim — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Esther doth declare.

Though the name of God be not found written in any verse of this Book, yet is His finger visible in every line. We see the wicked plot of Haman to utterly destroy the Jewish nation, and the wonderful way in which Queen Esther and her uncle Mordecai were raised up by Divine Providence for such a time as this. In the end, Haman is hanged upon the very gallows he had prepared for Mordecai, shewing how the Lord doth snare the wicked in the work of their own hands.

_score_detail.note: Nice 'snared in their own work' moral; calls Mordecai 'uncle' (commonly cousin/guardian).

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Job?

A profound and majestic poem treating of the great question of human suffering. Job, a man perfect and upright, is afflicted with the loss of children, wealth, and health, and must endure the disputations of his three friends who insist that his calamity must be punishment for sin. Job maintaineth his integrity, and at the last the Lord answereth out of the whirlwind, declaring His unfathomable wisdom. Job repenteth in dust and ashes, and the Lord restoreth his fortunes twofold.

_score_detail.note: Suffering, three friends, whirlwind, repentance, twofold restoration — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Psalms?

The Psalter, that great treasury of Hebrew devotion, containing an hundred and fifty songs of praise, lamentation, thanksgiving, and wisdom. Penned chiefly by David the sweet singer of Israel, with contributions from Asaph, the sons of Korah, and others, these sacred poems give voice to every motion of the soul toward God. From the cry of penitence in the fifty-first Psalm unto the exultant Hallelujahs of the closing songs, no book of Holy Writ hath more sustained the faithful through the ages.

_score_detail.note: 150 psalms, Davidic/Asaph authorship, range of devotion — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Psalms doth declare.

The holy and heavenly Treasury of the Church, containing those divine songs and prayers of King David and other saints. Herein are devotions suited for every estate of the soul: in sickness, in joy, in penitence, and in persecution. In our daily cathedral service in London, these Psalms do lift our hearts to heaven, full of sweet prophecies of our Savior’s passion, His resurrection, and His eternal kingdom. They are indeed the very anatomy of the Christian soul.

_score_detail.note: Beautiful 'anatomy of the Christian soul'; ties to cathedral use; fitting.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Proverbs?

A collection of wise sayings and moral instructions, chiefly from the pen of Solomon the son of David, to whom God gave wisdom exceeding all the men of the East. The fear of the Lord is declared to be the beginning of knowledge, and the reader is exhorted to seek wisdom as silver and understanding as hid treasure. Herein are precepts for the young, warnings against the strange woman, and the famous description of the virtuous woman whose price is far above rubies.

_score_detail.note: Solomonic wisdom, fear of the Lord, strange woman, virtuous woman — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Ecclesiastes, or The Preacher?

The meditations of the Preacher, the son of David, who applied his heart to search out all things done under the sun and found them vanity and vexation of spirit. With sombre eloquence he declareth that wisdom, pleasure, labour, and riches alike pass away, and that all men go unto the same end. Yet at the last he giveth the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God and keep His commandments, for this is the whole duty of man, and God shall bring every work into judgment.

_score_detail.note: Vanity, search under the sun, fear God conclusion — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Song of Solomon.

The Song of Songs, which is Solomon's, a lyric poem of surpassing beauty celebrating the love between the bridegroom and the bride. Interpreted by the faithful as an allegory of the love between Christ and His Church, its passionate imagery hath comforted and elevated the souls of believers who find therein the tender communion of the Redeemer with His redeemed. Many waters cannot quench love, neither can the floods drown it.

_score_detail.note: Christ/Church allegory, 'many waters cannot quench love' — accurate for the era.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Song of Solomon?

A most divine and mystical love song, which, though written in the warm language of earthly spouses, is to be understood spiritually and with a chaste mind. It is a celestial dialogue between Christ, the heavenly Bridegroom, and His beloved Spouse, the Church. In this book, we contemplate the unspeakable love, beauty, and mutual delight that existeth between our Savior and the souls of His elect, far exceeding all the transient loves of this world.

_score_detail.note: Period-perfect chaste allegorical reading; well expressed.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of the Prophet Isaiah doth declare.

The greatest of the writing prophets, Isaiah the son of Amoz saw visions concerning Judah and Jerusalem in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah. His book openeth with thunderous denunciation of sin and calls to repentance, yet shineth with the brightest Messianic promises in all the Old Testament: the virgin shall conceive and bear Immanuel; unto us a child is born; He was wounded for our transgressions. The latter chapters breathe comfort to the exiles and proclaim the new heavens and the new earth.

_score_detail.note: Kings dated, judgment, Immanuel/Suffering Servant, new heavens — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of the Prophet Jeremiah doth declare.

The weeping prophet, called from his mother's womb to speak the word of the Lord unto a rebellious nation. Jeremiah prophesied through the final days of Jerusalem, warning of the coming of Nebuchadnezzar and urging submission to the yoke of Babylon, for which he was persecuted, imprisoned, and cast into a miry dungeon. Yet in the midst of judgment he proclaimed the new covenant, when the Law should be written upon the heart, and the Lord would remember their sin no more.

_score_detail.note: Called from the womb, Babylon, persecution/dungeon, new covenant — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Lamentations of Jeremiah doth declare.

Five mournful elegies over the destruction of Jerusalem, composed in a meter of sorrow, each verse beginning with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet. The prophet weepeth over the city that once was great among the nations but now sitteth solitary. In the midst of desolation riseth a cry of hope: It is of the Lord's mercies that we are not consumed, because His compassions fail not; they are new every morning: great is Thy faithfulness.

_score_detail.note: Five acrostic elegies, 'great is Thy faithfulness' — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of the Prophet Ezekiel?

The prophecies of Ezekiel the priest, carried captive to Babylon, who saw visions of God by the river Chebar. His book openeth with the stupendous vision of the chariot-throne of the Almighty, borne by living creatures full of eyes. He pronounceth judgment upon Jerusalem and the nations, yet proclaimeth the promise of a new heart and a new spirit, the resurrection of the dry bones, and the vision of the restored Temple from which living waters flow, bringing healing to the nations.

_score_detail.note: Chariot-throne, judgment, new heart, dry bones, restored Temple — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Book of the Prophet Ezekiel.

The prophet Ezekiel, captive by the river of Chebar, beholds those high and terrible visions of the Chariot of the Cherubim, and the Glory of God departing from the temple. Yet he prophesieth the return of that Glory, the resurrection of the dry bones in the valley by the breath of the Holy Spirit, and the building of a glorious, mystical Temple, shewing that though the visible Church be laid low, yet God will surely restore her in His appointed time.

_score_detail.note: Glory departing/returning and dry bones well handled in cleric voice.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Daniel?

The book of Daniel, a prophet and statesman in the courts of Babylon and Persia, who interpreted the dreams of kings and was delivered from the den of lions. The first part relateth the faithfulness of Daniel and his companions in a heathen land: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego in the fiery furnace, and the handwriting on the wall at Belshazzar's feast. The latter part containeth visions of the rise and fall of empires, the coming of the Son of Man, and the promise of resurrection unto everlasting life.

_score_detail.note: Court tales and apocalyptic visions, Son of Man, resurrection — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Book of Hosea.

The word of the Lord that came unto Hosea, whose marriage to an unfaithful wife became a living parable of God's love for wayward Israel. Though the people had committed whoredom in departing from the Lord, yet He would allure her and speak comfortably unto her, betrothing her unto Himself in righteousness and judgment. The prophet calleth for repentance, declaring that the ways of the Lord are right, and the just shall walk in them.

_score_detail.note: Unfaithful wife as Israel, call to repentance, betrothal in righteousness — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Joel?

The prophecy of Joel, occasioned by a devastating plague of locusts which he declareth to be a portent of the great and terrible Day of the Lord. He calleth the people to rend their hearts and not their garments, and to turn unto the Lord with fasting and weeping. Yet he also foretelleth the outpouring of the Spirit upon all flesh, when sons and daughters shall prophesy, and whosoever calleth on the name of the Lord shall be delivered.

_score_detail.note: Locusts, Day of the Lord, rend your hearts, Spirit poured out — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Joel doth declare.

He foretelleth a terrible plague of locusts and a consuming drought as a figure of the Great Day of the Lord's judgment, calling upon the priests to weep between the porch and the altar, and to rent their hearts and not their garments. He then foretelleth that glorious outpouring of the Holy Ghost upon all flesh, which was fulfilled on the day of Pentecost, and the salvation of all who call upon the name of the Lord.

_score_detail.note: Rightly links the outpouring to Pentecost; good coverage.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Amos doth declare.

The words of Amos, a herdsman of Tekoa, whom the Lord called to prophesy against the nations and against Israel in a time of prosperity and corruption. With fearless plainness he denounceth the oppression of the poor, the bribery of judges, and the empty worship of those whose hearts are far from God. He declareth a famine not of bread but of hearing the words of the Lord, yet concludeth with the promise that the tabernacle of David shall be raised up and Israel planted in her land forever.

_score_detail.note: Herdsman of Tekoa, social injustice, famine of the word, tabernacle of David — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Amos?

A plain herdsman of Tekoa, whom the Lord did send to denounce the luxury, pride, and oppression of the poor in the kingdom of Israel. He mocketh at their hypocritical sacrifices while they did sell the righteous for silver and the poor for a pair of shoes. He warneth us that God will not accept our outward church-pomps if we neglect justice, and that righteousness must run down as a mighty stream.

_score_detail.note: 'Sell the poor for a pair of shoes' and justice-as-stream well used.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Obadiah?

The brief but weighty vision of Obadiah concerning Edom, the descendants of Esau, who rejoiced at the calamity of their brother Jacob and stood aloof when strangers carried away his substance. The prophet declareth the judgment of the Lord upon their pride and cruelty, and foretelleth the day when saviours shall come upon Mount Zion to judge the mount of Esau, and the kingdom shall be the Lord's.

_score_detail.note: Edom's pride and judgment, saviours on Zion — full for so short a book.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Obadiah?

In this brief yet heavy prophecy, the Edomites are denounced for their unnatural pride and the cruel joy they took in the desolation of their brother Jacob. They did stand in the crossway to cut off those that did escape. The prophet declareth that as Edom hath done, so shall it be done unto them, while upon Mount Zion there shall be deliverance, and the kingdom shall be the Lord's.

_score_detail.note: Captures Edom's standing-aloof and the reversal succinctly.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Jonah?

The remarkable history of Jonah the son of Amittai, who fled from the presence of the Lord rather than preach repentance unto Nineveh, that great city of the Assyrians. Swallowed by a great fish and delivered after three days, he at length obeyed the divine commission, and the Ninevites repented in sackcloth and ashes. The story endeth with the Lord's rebuke to the pouting prophet, shewing the breadth of divine mercy even unto the heathen who cannot discern their right hand from their left.

_score_detail.note: Flight, fish, Nineveh's repentance, the rebuke — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Jonah doth declare.

The marvelous history of Jonah, who, fleeing from his mission to heathen Nineveh, was overtaken by a tempest and swallowed by a great fish for three days and three nights. This was a direct and miraculous sign of our Savior’s burial and resurrection on the third day. His subsequent preaching in Nineveh and their general repentance shews that the mercy of God is not confined to the Jews, but is open to the penitent among the Gentiles.

_score_detail.note: Rightly notes the resurrection 'sign'; covers the arc.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Micah?

The word of the Lord that came to Micah the Morasthite, who prophesied against both Samaria and Jerusalem, denouncing the oppression of the poor by the rich, the false prophets who cry peace when they have wherewith to bite, and the corrupt rulers who build up Zion with blood. Yet he foretold that out of Bethlehem should come a Ruler in Israel whose goings forth have been from everlasting, and he concludeth with the glorious promise of a God who delighteth in mercy and will cast all our sins into the depths of the sea.

_score_detail.note: Oppression denounced, Bethlehem prophecy, 'do justly, love mercy' — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Micah?

This prophet denounceth the covetousness and oppression of the princes of Israel and Judah, but comforteth the faithful with the glorious prophecy that the Messiah, the Ruler of Israel, should be born in the little town of Bethlehem of Judah. He summeth up our Christian duty in those golden words: 'to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God.'

_score_detail.note: Bethlehem prophecy and the 'golden words' well handled.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Nahum?

The burden of Nineveh, a prophecy of unrelieved judgment against the capital of the Assyrian empire, whose cruelty had long afflicted the nations. Nahum declareth the Lord slow to anger and great in power, yet who will by no means clear the guilty. With vivid imagery he describeth the siege and fall of that proud city, whose wickedness had passed continually, and whose destruction none should mourn.

_score_detail.note: Burden of Nineveh, slow to anger yet just, fall of the city — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Book of Nahum.

A most lofty and poetic prophecy of the utter desolation and final ruin of Nineveh, the bloody city and capital of the Assyrian empire, which had long oppressed the people of God. It comforteth the Church, shewing that though the Lord be slow to anger and great in power, He is a stronghold in the day of trouble, and will not suffer the cruel oppressors of His people to stand.

_score_detail.note: Comfort-to-the-Church angle apt; covers the theme.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Habakkuk doth declare.

The burden which Habakkuk the prophet did see, a dialogue of deep searching between the prophet and his God. He cryeth out against the violence and injustice that go unpunished, and the Lord answereth that He will raise up the Chaldeans, a bitter and hasty nation, to execute judgment. The prophet trembleth at the vision yet concludeth with a matchless confession of faith: Though the fig tree shall not blossom, yet will I rejoice in the Lord; I will joy in the God of my salvation.

_score_detail.note: Dialogue, Chaldeans raised up, 'just shall live by faith', closing song — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Book of Habakkuk.

The prophet Habakkuk pleadeth with the Lord concerning the violence of the wicked and the coming of the Chaldeans to scourge Judah. He is answered that the vision is for an appointed time, and that 'the just shall live by his faith'—a sacred truth which lies at the very foundation of our Protestant religion. He concludeth with a most excellent song of praise, rejoicing in the Lord though the fig tree should not blossom.

_score_detail.note: Rightly highlights 'the just shall live by faith' as a Protestant cornerstone.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Zephaniah doth declare.

The word of the Lord by Zephaniah in the days of good King Josiah, prophesying the great Day of the Lord, a day of wrath and trouble and distress, when the Lord shall search Jerusalem with candles and punish the men that are settled on their lees. Yet the tone changeth to one of glorious hope: the Lord is in the midst of thee, a mighty one who will save; He will rejoice over thee with joy; He will rest in His love; He will joy over thee with singing.

_score_detail.note: Day of wrath, searching Jerusalem with candles, then the song of joy — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Zephaniah?

He denounceth the severe judgments of God upon Judah for her idolatry, her swearing by Malcham, and her conforming to the strange apparel of the heathen. Yet, in the end, he turneth his voice to sweet comfort, promising that the Lord will gather those that are sorrowful for the solemn assembly, and make Zion a name and a praise among all people of the earth.

_score_detail.note: Malcham/strange-apparel detail shows close reading; good coverage.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Haggai doth declare.

The prophecies of Haggai, delivered in the second year of Darius, exhorting the returned exiles to arise and build the house of the Lord, which lay neglected while every man dwelt in his own ceiled house. He declareth that the silver and the gold are the Lord's, and that the glory of this latter house shall be greater than the former. His words, accompanied by the leadership of Zerubbabel and Joshua, stirred the people to complete the second Temple.

_score_detail.note: Rebuke for neglect, latter house glory, Zerubbabel/Joshua — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Haggai doth declare.

After the return from Babylon, the people neglected the rebuilding of the temple, choosing rather to build their own ceiled houses. Haggai reproveth their coldness, shewing that their droughts and poor crops were the punishments of their neglect of God’s house. He exhorteth them to be strong and build, promising that the glory of this latter house should exceed that of the former, which was fulfilled when Christ entered it.

_score_detail.note: 'Ceiled houses' detail and Christ-entering fulfilment well noted.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Zechariah?

The visions of Zechariah, companion of Haggai, speaking to the returned exiles. His book openeth with a series of night visions: the man among the myrtle trees, the four horns and four carpenters, the flying roll, the woman in the ephah. He prophesieth of the coming King, lowly and riding upon an ass, who shall speak peace unto the nations. He foretelleth the smiting of the Shepherd and the scattering of the sheep, and the day when they shall look upon Him whom they have pierced.

_score_detail.note: Night visions, coming King on an ass, smitten Shepherd, pierced one — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Book of Zechariah doth declare.

A contemporary of Haggai, who did encourage the builders of the Temple with many deep and mysterious visions. He foretelleth with marvelous detail the humble entry of our Savior into Jerusalem, riding upon an ass, His betrayal for thirty pieces of silver, His being pierced by those of His own house, and the opening of a Fountain in Jerusalem for sin and for uncleanness.

_score_detail.note: Messianic detail (30 pieces, piercing, fountain) shows real knowledge of the text.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Book of Malachi?

The final prophetic voice of the Old Testament, a series of disputations between the Lord and a people grown weary and negligent in their worship. Malachi rebuketh the priests for offering polluted bread upon the altar, condemneth the treachery of divorce, and calleth the people to bring the tithes into the storehouse. He foretelleth the coming of Elijah the prophet before the great and dreadful day of the Lord, and so the canon closeth with a promise and a warning, awaiting the dawn of the new dispensation.

_score_detail.note: Polluted offerings, divorce, tithes, Elijah to come — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Book of Malachi?

The last of the Old Testament prophets, who reproveth both priests and people for their corrupt sacrifices, their divorces, and their robbing God of tithes. He foretelleth the sending of John the Baptist in the spirit of Elijah to prepare the way, and the sudden coming of the Messiah, the Sun of Righteousness, with healing in His wings, to purge his Temple before the great and dreadful day of the Lord.

_score_detail.note: Sun of Righteousness and Baptist-as-Elijah well handled.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Gospel According to Saint Matthew?

The Gospel according to Saint Matthew, written primarily for the Hebrew people, shewing how Jesus of Nazareth fulfilleth all the prophecies of the Old Testament concerning the Messiah. It beginneth with the genealogy from Abraham, relateth the Sermon on the Mount with its beatitudes, and setteth forth the parables of the kingdom. It giveth full account of the Passion, death, and glorious Resurrection of our Lord, and concludeth with the Great Commission: Go ye therefore and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost.

_score_detail.note: Genealogy, Sermon, parables, Passion/Resurrection, Great Commission — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Gospel According to Saint Mark?

The Gospel of Saint Mark, the most brief and urgent of the four, portraying our Lord as the suffering Servant who came not to be ministered unto but to minister. It moveth swiftly from the baptism in Jordan through the Galilean ministry of healing and exorcism unto the final week in Jerusalem. The narrative is vivid and immediate, shewing forth the authority of the Son of God over demons, disease, and death itself, and recounting His betrayal, Crucifixion, and triumphant rising from the tomb.

_score_detail.note: Brief/urgent, suffering Servant, authority over demons/disease/death — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Gospel According to Saint Luke.

The Gospel according to the beloved physician, Saint Luke, addressed to the most excellent Theophilus, being a careful and orderly account of the life and ministry of our Saviour. It alone preserveth the beautiful nativity canticles, the parables of the Good Samaritan and the Prodigal Son, and the story of the disciples on the road to Emmaus. Luke presenteth Christ as the compassionate friend of sinners, the seeker and saviour of the lost, whose salvation is prepared before the face of all people, a light to lighten the Gentiles.

_score_detail.note: Theophilus, nativity canticles, Good Samaritan/Prodigal, Emmaus, friend of sinners — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Gospel According to Saint John.

The Gospel of Saint John, the disciple whom Jesus loved, soaring in its theological heights above the synoptics as an eagle above the earth. It openeth with the sublime declaration that in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. Herein are the great I AM sayings of our Lord, the discourse on the bread of life, the raising of Lazarus, and the high-priestly prayer in the upper room. It was written that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing ye might have life through His name.

_score_detail.note: Logos prologue, I AM sayings, Lazarus, high-priestly prayer, purpose statement — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Acts of the Apostles doth declare.

The Acts of the Apostles, penned by the same Luke, recordeth the birth of the Christian Church at Pentecost, when the Holy Ghost descended as a rushing mighty wind and tongues of fire sat upon the disciples. It followeth the early preaching of Peter in Jerusalem, the martyrdom of Stephen, and the conversion of Saul of Tarsus who became Paul, the great apostle to the Gentiles. The narrative of his three missionary journeys, his trials before governors and kings, and his voyage to Rome concludeth with him preaching the kingdom of God with all confidence, no man forbidding him.

_score_detail.note: Pentecost, Stephen, Saul's conversion, journeys, Rome — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Romans?

The epistle of Paul to the church at Rome, the most comprehensive exposition of Christian doctrine in all Scripture. The apostle declareth that all have sinned and come short of the glory of God, and that a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law. He unfoldeth the doctrines of election, sanctification, and the future of Israel, and he concludeth with practical exhortations to present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable unto God. To God only wise be glory through Jesus Christ forever.

_score_detail.note: All have sinned, justification by faith, election, living sacrifice — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Romans doth declare.

Saint Paul's masterwork of theology, wherein he systematically unfoldeth the great doctrine of Justification by Faith alone, without the works of the Law. He proveth both Jew and Gentile to be under original sin and guilt, and that our only righteousness is the imputed righteousness of Christ. He handleth the deep mysteries of Election and Predestination, and closeth with the duties of the Christian in the commonwealth.

_score_detail.note: Solid Reformed reading of justification, imputation, election.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Corinthians?

Paul's first epistle to the church at Corinth, a congregation rent by divisions and beset by carnality, yet rich in spiritual gifts. The apostle addresseth questions of marriage, meats offered to idols, the Lord's Supper, and the resurrection of the dead. In the thirteenth chapter he giveth that peerless hymn to charity which surpasseth all knowledge and prophecy. Throughout he exhorteth the brethren to do all things decently and in order, and to stand fast in the faith.

_score_detail.note: Divisions, marriage/idol-meat, Supper, charity, resurrection — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Corinthians?

An epistle of wholesome discipline and correction, written by Paul to the unruly church of Corinth. He rebuketh their divisions, their pride, the toleration of incest, and their disorders at the Lord’s Table. He highly commendeth the grace of Charity or Love, and delivereth that most glorious and majestic defense of the Resurrection of the body, which we read at our gravesides in the burial office.

_score_detail.note: Notes the burial-office use of ch.15; covers the issues.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Second Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Corinthians?

The second epistle to Corinth, the most personal of Paul's letters, written in defence of his apostolic authority against false teachers who had troubled the church. He openeth his heart concerning his sufferings, his thorn in the flesh, and his sincere love for the brethren. He commendeth the grace of giving, warneth against being unequally yoked with unbelievers, and boasteth in his infirmities that the power of Christ may rest upon him. The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the communion of the Holy Ghost be with you all.

_score_detail.note: Defence of apostleship, sufferings, thorn, giving, unequal yoke — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Second Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Corinthians doth declare.

Herein Saint Paul defendeth his apostolic authority against false teachers, opening his very heart to the Corinthians. He recounteth his manifold sufferings, his shipwreck, his scourging, and his thorn in the flesh, shewing that the strength of Christ is made perfect in our weakness, and that we have this treasure of the gospel in earthen vessels, that the glory may be of God.

_score_detail.note: 'Treasure in earthen vessels' and strength-in-weakness well drawn.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Galatians.

An epistle of fierce urgency, wherein Paul contendeth for the liberty of the Gospel against those who would impose circumcision and the yoke of the Mosaic Law upon Gentile believers. He declareth that a man is not justified by the works of the law but by the faith of Jesus Christ, and that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, bond nor free, male nor female. The fruit of the Spirit is set against the works of the flesh, and the believer is exhorted to walk in newness of life.

_score_detail.note: Liberty vs circumcision, justification by faith, fruit of the Spirit — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Galatians doth declare.

The great Bulwark of Christian Liberty against those Judaisers who sought to bring the Galatians under the yoke of the ceremonial law. Saint Paul vehemently declareth that if any man, or even an angel from heaven, preach another gospel than justification by faith in Christ alone, he is accursed. This is the very book wherewith our Protestant Reformers did break the chains of Popery.

_score_detail.note: 'Bulwark of Christian Liberty against Popery' is a perfect 1699 framing.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Ephesians?

A sublime epistle treating of the eternal purpose of God to gather together in one all things in Christ. Paul declareth that we are saved by grace through faith, not of works, and unfoldeth the mystery of the Church as the body of Christ and the temple of the Holy Ghost. The latter portion containeth the armour of God, that the believer may stand against the wiles of the devil. Peace be to the brethren, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

_score_detail.note: Eternal purpose, grace through faith, Church as body, armour of God — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Philippians?

The epistle of joy, penned by Paul from his bonds in Rome unto the beloved church at Philippi. He rejoiceth that Christ is preached, whether in pretence or in truth, and setteth forth the great kenosis: Christ Jesus, being in the form of God, made Himself of no reputation and became obedient unto death. He exhorteth the brethren to rejoice in the Lord always, to be careful for nothing, and to press toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus.

_score_detail.note: Joy from prison, kenosis hymn, press toward the mark — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Philippians doth declare.

Written from a Roman prison, this is an epistle of sweet, heavenly joy and affection. Paul exhorteth the Philippians to unity and humility, setting before them the great pattern of Christ, who humbled Himself to the death of the Cross. He declareth that for him to live is Christ, and to die is gain, teaching us to rejoice in the Lord alway, in whatsoever state we are.

_score_detail.note: 'To live is Christ, to die is gain' and the humility-pattern well used.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Colossians?

An epistle exalting the supremacy of Christ, who is the image of the invisible God, by whom all things were created and in whom all fulness dwelleth. The apostle warneth against philosophy and vain deceit, the worship of angels, and the traditions of men. He exhorteth the Colossians to set their affections on things above, to put off the old man with his deeds, and to let the word of Christ dwell in them richly in all wisdom.

_score_detail.note: Supremacy of Christ, warning against philosophy, things above — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Colossians.

In this divine letter, Paul warneth the Colossians against the dangerous mixtures of heathen philosophy and Jewish ceremonies which did seek to rob Christ of His glory. He declareth Him to be the Image of the invisible God, the Creator of all things, in whom dwelleth all the fullness of the Godhead bodily, and who hath made peace through the blood of His Cross.

_score_detail.note: Fulness of the Godhead and peace by the blood well captured.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Thessalonians.

The earliest of Paul's epistles, written to a young church suffering persecution and troubled by questions concerning those who had fallen asleep in the Lord. The apostle comforteth them with the assurance that the dead in Christ shall rise first, and that we which remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air. He exhorteth them to holiness, to study to be quiet, and to comfort one another with these words.

_score_detail.note: Earliest epistle, dead in Christ rise, caught up, exhortations — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Thessalonians.

A very warm and comfortable epistle to a young Church, wherein Paul praiseth their faith, love, and patience under tribulation. He highly comforteth them concerning those who have fallen asleep in Jesus, assuring them that at the last trump, the dead in Christ shall rise first, and we shall be caught up together in the clouds to meet the Lord.

_score_detail.note: Comfort about those asleep and the rapture well handled.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Second Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Thessalonians?

A brief epistle correcting misunderstandings concerning the Day of the Lord, which some believed had already come. Paul teacheth that a falling away must first occur and the man of sin be revealed before that great day. He exhorteth the brethren to stand fast and hold the traditions they have been taught, and to withdraw themselves from every brother that walketh disorderly. The Lord of peace Himself give you peace always by all means.

_score_detail.note: Day of the Lord not yet come, falling away, man of sin, stand fast — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Second Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Thessalonians doth declare.

Paul writeth to correct a mistake among them concerning the immediate coming of the Day of Christ. He revealeth that there must first come a great Apostasy or falling away, and the manifestation of the Man of Sin, the Son of Perdition, who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God—which we in England do know to be the Roman Papacy.

_score_detail.note: Identifies the Man of Sin with the Roman Papacy — quintessentially 1699 Protestant.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Timothy?

The first pastoral epistle, wherein Paul instructeth his son in the faith, Timothy, concerning the ordering of the church at Ephesus. He giveth qualifications for bishops and deacons, warneth against false teachers and fables, and prescribeth the proper conduct of men and women in the congregation. This is a faithful saying and worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am chief.

_score_detail.note: Church order, bishops/deacons, false teachers, 'Christ came to save sinners' — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Timothy?

A pastoral epistle containing Paul’s fatherly instructions to his son Timothy for the government of the Church. He outlines the holy qualifications of Bishops and Deacons, the ordering of public prayer, and the care of widows. He warneth against those who depart from the faith, and delivereth that great warning that the love of money is the root of all evil.

_score_detail.note: 'Love of money the root of all evil' and office qualifications well noted.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Second Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Timothy doth declare.

The swan song of the aged apostle, written from his final imprisonment in Rome when the time of his departure was at hand. Paul exhorteth Timothy to stir up the gift of God, to endure hardness as a good soldier, and to preach the word in season and out of season. He declareth that he hath fought a good fight, finished the course, and kept the faith, and that henceforth there is laid up for him a crown of righteousness. The Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit.

_score_detail.note: Final imprisonment, 'preach the word', 'fought a good fight', crown — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Second Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Timothy.

Paul’s dying testament and charge to Timothy, written from his cold dungeon in Rome as he awaited the axe of Nero. He exhorteth him to be strong, to endure hardness as a good soldier of Jesus Christ, to preach the word in season and out of season, and to hold fast to the holy scriptures, which are able to make us wise unto salvation.

_score_detail.note: Vivid 'cold dungeon... axe of Nero'; covers the charge well.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Titus.

A pastoral letter to Titus, left in Crete to set in order the things that were wanting and to ordain elders in every city. Paul giveth instruction for aged men and women, young men, and servants, exhorting all to adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things. He declareth that the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men, teaching us to live soberly and righteously in this present world, looking for that blessed hope and glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ.

_score_detail.note: Crete, ordaining elders, sound doctrine, grace teaching sober living — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Titus doth declare.

A pastoral epistle to Titus, whom Paul had left in Crete to set in order the things that were wanting and to ordain Elders in every city. He exhorteth him to preach sound doctrine, showing that the grace of God which bringeth salvation teacheth us to live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present evil world, looking for that blessed hope.

_score_detail.note: Grace-that-brings-salvation and blessed hope well handled.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Philemon?

The briefest of Paul's epistles, a personal letter of exquisite tact and charity unto Philemon, a beloved fellow labourer, on behalf of Onesimus, a runaway slave whom Paul had converted in his Roman bonds. The apostle beseecheth Philemon to receive Onesimus no longer as a servant but as a brother beloved, offering to repay any debt from his own hand. It is a living demonstration of the power of the Gospel to transform the bonds of master and servant into the communion of saints.

_score_detail.note: Onesimus the runaway, brother-not-servant, Paul's offer to repay — full.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Philemon?

A beautiful, short letter which is a perfect model of Christian courtesy and love. Paul intercedeth for Onesimus, a runaway slave who had robbed his master Philemon, but had been converted under Paul’s bonds. Paul entreateth Philemon to receive him back, no longer as a servant, but as a brother beloved, offering to repay whatever Onesimus did owe.

_score_detail.note: Model of Christian courtesy; covers the whole short letter.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Hebrews.

An epistle of matchless eloquence, shewing forth the surpassing glory of Christ above the angels, above Moses, and above the Aaronic priesthood. The author declareth that Christ is the mediator of a better covenant, established upon better promises, having entered once for all into the holy place by His own blood. The eleventh chapter is the great roll-call of faith, and the twelfth exhorteth us to run with patience the race set before us, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith.

_score_detail.note: Christ above angels/Moses/priesthood, better covenant, roll-call of faith — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Hebrews?

An incomparable, divine treatise, wherein the writer shews the infinite superiority of our Lord Jesus Christ over Angels, over Moses, and over the Levitical Priesthood. He demonstrates that the sacrifices of the old Law were but temporary shadows, and that Christ is our eternal High Priest, who hath entered the holy place once for all, having obtained eternal redemption for us.

_score_detail.note: Superiority of Christ and eternal High Priest well drawn.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the General Epistle of James?

The epistle of James, the Lord's brother, practical and penetrating, addressed to the twelve tribes scattered abroad. He teacheth that faith without works is dead, and that pure religion is to visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction and to keep oneself unspotted from the world. He warneth against the unbridled tongue, against respect of persons, and against the love of riches. The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much.

_score_detail.note: Faith without works dead, pure religion, the tongue, fervent prayer — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the First Epistle General of Peter?

The first epistle of Peter, the apostle who denied his Lord and was restored, written to the suffering churches of Asia Minor. He comforteth them with the assurance that they are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, and exhorteth them to endure suffering with patience after the example of Christ, who suffered for us, the just for the unjust. He warneth that the devil walketh about as a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour, and calleth the elders to feed the flock of God.

_score_detail.note: Chosen generation/royal priesthood, suffering, roaring lion, feed the flock — accurate.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the First Epistle General of Peter?

An epistle full of heavenly consolation, written to the scattered and persecuted Christians of the first age. Peter exhorteth them to stand fast under the fiery trial of suffering, to walk in holiness, to fear God and honor the King, and to commit their souls to Him in well-doing, as unto a faithful Creator, remembering the sufferings of our Lord.

_score_detail.note: Fiery-trial endurance and 'fear God, honour the King' well noted.

messages.content: What is the general matter and argument of the Second Epistle General of Peter?

The swan song of the aged Peter, written shortly before his martyrdom, as a parting exhortation to grow in grace and knowledge. He warneth against false teachers who privily bring in damnable heresies, and he answereth the scoffers who ask where is the promise of His coming. The heavens and the earth which are now are reserved unto fire against the day of judgment, but we, according to His promise, look for new heavens and a new earth wherein dwelleth righteousness.

_score_detail.note: Grow in grace, false teachers, scoffers, elements melt, new heavens — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Second Epistle General of Peter.

Saint Peter's dying letter, wherein he warneth the Church against those scoffing heretics and false teachers of the last days who deny the promise of Christ’s second coming. He declares the absolute certainty of the holy scriptures, and foretelleth that the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night, wherein the elements shall melt with fervent heat.

_score_detail.note: Scripture's certainty and the thief-in-the-night day well handled.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the First Epistle General of John?

The first epistle of John the beloved, breathing the air of intimate communion with God. He declareth that God is light and in Him is no darkness, that God is love, and that perfect love casteth out fear. He giveth the tests of true faith: confession of Christ come in the flesh, love of the brethren, and the indwelling witness of the Spirit. These things are written that ye may know that ye have eternal life, even unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God.

_score_detail.note: God is light/love, perfect love casts out fear, tests of faith — accurate.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the First Epistle General of John.

A most spiritual and comforting epistle of love, penned by the aged Apostle John. He giveth us sure marks whereby we may know that we are passed from death unto life, chiefly our love to the brethren and our keeping of His commandments. He warneth against antichristian deceivers, and assureth us that 'God is love', and that His Son’s blood cleanseth us from all sin.

_score_detail.note: Marks of passing from death to life and 'God is love' well drawn.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Second Epistle of John.

A brief epistle from the elder unto the elect lady and her children, a message of love and warning. He rejoiceth that they walk in truth and exhorteth them to love one another. He solemnly warneth that many deceivers are entered into the world who confess not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh, and biddeth the faithful not to receive such into their houses, lest they be partakers of their evil deeds.

_score_detail.note: Elect lady, walk in truth, deceivers denying Christ in flesh, receive them not — full.

messages.content: I prithee, good sir, set forth for me a brief rehearsal of all that is written in the Second Epistle of John.

A brief and affectionate letter written by the Elder to a Christian Lady of renown and her children, rejoicing in their walking in the truth, and warning them not to receive into their houses or bid godspeed to those heretics who deny that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh.

_score_detail.note: Captures the whole brief letter accurately.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Third Epistle of John doth declare.

A personal letter unto the wellbeloved Gaius, commending his hospitality toward the travelling brethren who went forth for the name's sake. The elder contrasteth the faithful service of Demetrius with the pride of Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence and receiveth not the brethren. Beloved, follow not that which is evil but that which is good; he that doeth good is of God.

_score_detail.note: Gaius's hospitality, Demetrius vs Diotrephes, follow good — full.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Third Epistle of John?

A short letter to the beloved Gaius, whom the Apostle commendeth for his hospitality to the traveling preachers of the Gospel. He sharply rebuketh the proud and tyrannical Diotrephes, who loved to have the preeminence among them, prating against the Apostles with malicious words and casting the brethren out of the Church.

_score_detail.note: Diotrephes-the-preeminence-lover well captured.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the General Epistle of Jude?

A short but fervent epistle from Jude, the brother of James, exhorting the faithful to contend earnestly for the faith once delivered unto the saints. He warneth against ungodly men who have crept in unawares, turning the grace of God into lasciviousness, and he setteth forth the fearful judgments that befell the unbelieving Israelites, the fallen angels, and the cities of Sodom and Gomorrha. Now unto Him that is able to keep you from falling, to the only wise God our Saviour, be glory and majesty, dominion and power, both now and ever.

_score_detail.note: Contend for the faith, crept-in ungodly men, OT judgments, doxology — accurate.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the General Epistle of Jude doth declare.

A short, fiery epistle, wherein the apostle Jude exhorteth the saints to contend earnestly for the faith which was once delivered to them. He painteth in dark colors the character and terrible doom of those filthy dreamers and mockers who have crept into the Church unawares, turning the grace of our God into lasciviousness.

_score_detail.note: 'Filthy dreamers' turning grace to lasciviousness well drawn; omits the OT examples.

messages.content: Make known unto me, in few words, what the Revelation of Saint John the Divine doth declare.

The Revelation of Jesus Christ given unto Saint John the Divine upon the isle of Patmos, the only book of prophecy in the New Testament. It openeth with letters to the seven churches of Asia, proceedeth through visions of seals and trumpets and vials of wrath, and cometh unto the final triumph of the Lamb: the marriage supper, the binding of Satan, the great white throne judgment, and the descent of the New Jerusalem, that holy city wherein God shall dwell with men and wipe away all tears. The Spirit and the bride say, Come. Even so, come, Lord Jesus.

_score_detail.note: Seven churches, seals/trumpets/vials, Lamb's triumph, New Jerusalem — accurate.

messages.content: What are the principal things recorded in the Revelation of Saint John the Divine?

The sublime and dreadful Apocalypse of Saint John, written in his banishment upon the isle of Patmos. It contains those high and mysterious visions of the seven golden candlesticks, the four horsemen, the fall of mystical Babylon (which our Protestant divines have long identified with the Roman Papacy), the binding of the Dragon, and the final descent of the beautiful New Jerusalem, where all tears shall be wiped away.

_score_detail.note: Identifies mystical Babylon with the Papacy — characteristically 1699 Protestant; well done.
